Strategic security alignment

TECO Energy / Tampa Electric

Executive brief · Splunk Enterprise Security · April 10, 2026

Executive summary

TECO Energy operates critical infrastructure for 825,000+ customers in West Central Florida with a lean security organization within a ~3,300–3,700 employee enterprise. A multi-billion-dollar grid modernization program, tighter NERC CIP expectations, and nation-state interest in U.S. utilities increase pressure to scale detection, investigation, and response without proportional hiring. This brief maps strategic objectives to Splunk Enterprise Security (ES) Essentials and ES Premier, with a 15-month phased path.

Bottom line: ES Essentials delivers risk-based alerting, threat intelligence management, Detection Studio, AI Assistant in Security, and Exposure Analytics. ES Premier adds native SOAR (unlimited seats), UEBA, Attack Analyzer, and unified TDIR for automated response and advanced threats—timed after foundational SIEM and data discipline.

Strategic objectives → Splunk capabilities

Strategic objective Current challenge ES Essentials capability Expected outcome
Risk-based detection prioritization (IT/OT) Alert overload; hard to separate critical OT risks from noise Risk-Based Alerting (RBA) Major reduction in alert noise; focus on high-fidelity incidents tied to asset criticality
Intelligence-driven detection Manual IOC work; gaps vs. utility-focused campaigns Threat Intelligence Management (TIM) STIX/TAXII, CISA ICS advisories, E-ISAC feeds operationalized; faster time-to-detect
Scalable detection lifecycle Rule sprawl; uneven coverage vs. MITRE ATT&CK for ICS / NIST CSF 2.0 Detection Studio Plan, test, deploy, and retire detections with measurable framework alignment
AI-accelerated investigation Analyst capacity limits; investigations are SPL- and time-heavy AI Assistant in Security Natural-language SPL, summaries, triage assistance; shorter mean-time-to-investigate
Continuous compliance monitoring Manual asset tracking; NERC CIP scope grows with modernization Exposure Analytics Asset discovery, identity enrichment, control-gap reporting; stronger audit readiness

Five core challenges (Essentials)

Alert overload (IT/OT): Modernization and solar expansion drive high alert volume. RBA replaces flat thresholds with risk scored by asset and identity context.

Threat intel without a large intel team: Sector feeds are voluminous. TIM ingests and normalizes STIX/TAXII, E-ISAC, CISA ICS, and commercial sources for actionable matching.

Detection sprawl: New assets need governed lifecycle. Detection Studio ties engineering to MITRE ATT&CK for ICS and NIST CSF 2.0 coverage and retirement.

Investigation capacity: AI Assistant in Security accelerates SPL, narrative summaries, and reports suitable for escalation and regulatory evidence.

NERC CIP and expanding scope: Exposure Analytics supports continuous asset visibility and deficiency visibility as critical cyber assets grow.

ES Premier — beyond Essentials

When TECO is ready to industrialize response and advanced detection, ES Premier extends the platform:

Advanced challenge ES Premier capability Expected outcome
Manual playbooks cannot match grid-scale events Native SOAR (unlimited seats) Multi-step containment across firewalls, endpoints, identity, OT integrations; faster MTTR
Insider and credential abuse blind spots Native UEBA Behavioral baselines for privileged and sensitive access patterns
Fragmented detect-to-respond workflow Unified TDIR Single flow for detect–investigate–respond–manage with consistent metrics
Low-and-slow APT and lateral movement Multi-layered detection (correlation + UEBA + AI) Campaigns surfaced through behavior, not signatures alone
Evasive malware and unknown payloads Attack Analyzer Dynamic analysis of files and URLs; verdicts fed back into workflows

Phased implementation roadmap

Phase Window Focus & success themes
1 — Foundation Months 1–3 RBA with asset weighting; TIM for sector feeds; baseline MTTI, alert volume, capacity
2 — Detection & investigation Months 4–6 Detection Studio governance and framework mapping; AI Assistant in analyst workflows; coverage reporting
3 — Compliance & visibility Months 7–9 Exposure Analytics; integrate inventory with detection roadmap; monthly compliance reporting
4 — Advanced TDIR Months 10–15 SOAR playbooks, UEBA, Attack Analyzer, unified TDIR; MTTR and insider-detection goals

Illustrative benchmark metrics

Figures below are industry-style benchmarks for planning discussions—confirm with local baselines before commitments.

Metric Typical baseline (range) ES Essentials direction ES Premier direction
Daily alert volume Very high Large reduction via RBA and tuning Further reduction + automation
MTTD / MTTI / MTTR Hours Materially faster for prioritized incidents Strongest gains with SOAR and unified TDIR
Analyst leverage Baseline FTE load AI + workflow discipline Automation + behavior analytics
NERC CIP audit prep Heavy periodic effort Better evidence and visibility More playbooks and continuous monitoring
Framework coverage Partial mapping Majority mapped and governed High coverage with continuous expansion
Planning note: Treat percentage improvement claims in stakeholder decks as hypotheses until validated against pilot data from TECO’s environment.

Business outcomes (summary)

  • Operational resilience: Faster detection and containment for threats that could affect the grid.
  • Regulatory & audit: Continuous monitoring and structured evidence for NERC CIP and stakeholder reporting.
  • Workforce: AI-assisted workflows and automation reduce burnout risk on a lean team.
  • Cost avoidance: Better leverage of existing analysts vs. linear hiring for 24/7 coverage.
  • Advanced threats: Layered analytics address campaigns that evade signatures alone.

Critical success factors

CISO / security leadership

  • Executive sponsorship tied to grid modernization and security investment milestones.
  • Joint governance with IT, OT engineering, regulatory affairs, and audit.
  • Early SOC involvement in Detection Studio and AI Assistant design.

CIO / IT leadership

  • Centralized data architecture for IT, OT, identity, and cloud telemetry; retention aligned to regulatory needs (e.g., multi-year audit trails where required).
  • Integration plan for firewalls, EDR, identity, OT security platforms, and Phase 1 automation targets.
  • Change management: realistic ramp for analysts adopting new workflows.

Cyber security leadership

  • Documented detection engineering lifecycle (plan, review, QA, retire) aligned to MITRE and NIST CSF 2.0.
  • Accountability for threat-feed quality and quarterly review of IOC relevance.
  • Published SOC SLAs for MTTI, MTTR, and coverage—with leadership visibility.

Conclusion

TECO faces convergent pressures: grid modernization, utility-targeted threats, NERC CIP, and a lean security team. Splunk ES Essentials provides an AI-ready SIEM foundation—RBA, TIM, Detection Studio, AI Assistant, and Exposure Analytics—to investigate and report faster with clearer framework alignment. ES Premier adds SOAR, UEBA, Attack Analyzer, and unified TDIR when the organization is ready to scale automated response and behavioral detection. A phased 15-month roadmap sequences foundation, detection lifecycle, compliance visibility, and advanced TDIR so capability tracks capital programs and regulatory expectations—scaling SecOps with infrastructure, not only with headcount.

Prepared for: TECO Energy / Tampa Electric leadership (CISO, CIO, Cyber Security leadership)

Document date: April 10, 2026
